g.data-narration: Welcome to Message Queues - a fundamental concept in distributed systems that enables asynchronous communication.
A message queue is a temporary storage mechanism where messages wait until consumed and processed by receiving applications.
Message queues decouple producers from consumers, enabling independent scaling, better reliability, and improved resource utilization.
Producers create and send messages to queues, while consumers retrieve and process them. They're completely independent of each other.
Message brokers like RabbitMQ, Kafka, and ActiveMQ manage message storage, routing, and delivery between producers and consumers.
g.text: Broker manages routing, storage, and delivery

g.data-narration: In Point-to-Point, each message is delivered to exactly one consumer. Used for task distribution and workflow processing.
In Pub/Sub, messages are broadcast to all subscribers. Perfect for event-driven architectures and real-time notifications.
g.text: All subscribers receive every message broadcast to the topic

g.data-narration: Persistent messages survive broker failures by being written to durable storage. Trade-off: reliability adds latency to message delivery.
Dead Letter Queues store messages that fail processing after multiple attempts. Essential for debugging and recovering from processing errors.
Multiple consumers enable parallel processing and automatic load distribution. Add more consumers to handle increased throughput.
g.text: Horizontal scaling: Add consumers to increase throughput

g.data-narration: E-commerce orders use queues to send confirmation emails. The order service queues emails without waiting for delivery completion.
Food delivery apps use multiple queues for each processing stage. Each service works independently at its own pace.
Key takeaways: Message queues enable asynchronous communication, support P2P and Pub/Sub patterns, ensure reliability through persistence.
g.text: Message queues are essential for microservices and cloud-native architectures

g.data-narration: For exams: Understand the difference between sync and async communication, know P2P vs Pub/Sub, and remember key implementations.
